rating 2.0. RIP - It stopped working after 2 weeks, then started, then finally died. Set up was not too difficult, if you are semi-experienced with assembling things. The instructions were pretty worthless, so I recommend finding an assembly video on YouTube. However, with that being said, do not be discouraged about getting this Anet ET4x printer. At the time I bought it, the price was $189. Pretty good price. It is a great entry level 3D printer that my 4th grade daughter and I are having a blast with. Once you get it assembled and the bed/table leveled to the print head, you can load some files and get printing. I recommend going to Thingiverse.com and finding models to download and print. If I had to start from scratch and do it over I would probably get this Anet ET4X again. I like it and it seems to be fairly easy to use once you get it put together. Have fun with it., I'm gonna go print a Baby Yoda now!

4/25/20 Update: Printer is printing great! It took me a little time to figure out the best general use settings for PLA filament. For regular printing I use layer height 0.1mm (sometimes 0.2 for faster print), print speed 70 mm/sec, filament temp 210° C, bed temp 70°C. I am new at this so I am still learning and adjusting settings. But so far the printer exceeds my expectations and is accurate. Just be sure to use a slicer software that lists this printer. The Cura 14.07 software that comes with the printer works great. I tried the newer version of Cura and it didn't list the Anet ET4X so it didn't work right.

Update 5/3/20: So, things were going smoothly until the hotend stopped being hot. It would heat up like normal and start printing but after about an hour or so into the print the hotend quit. The extruder feeder started grinding the filament because the hotend would not melt the filament. It looks like another reviewer here has had a similar issue with the hotend going bad. I tried to adjust the temperature on the touchscreen but it wouldn't respond and the setting would only stay at 0°. It's a bummer. I really liked this machine. I am going to have to return it.

rating 2.0. I bought into the hype..... I have to admit the videos and marketing of this product are awesome.  The ideas and process that he uses to diagnose do work and are sound. rating 5.0. Excellent Reference tool for novice and expert alike! This book is a great tool for anyone who has anything to do with electrical automotive, which in today's world is anyone who works on automotive equipment. I have a background in electronics and troubleshooting going back to my time in the Army as a communications maintainer, all the way through to my work now as an heavy equipment mobile mechanic for a military installation. This book had me hooked from the first page because of its simple, no b.s. layout. Dan gives practical examples and explanations with diagrams all throughout the guide. Not too much time spent on theory other than to explain it clear enough for the reader to grasp it and help them do their job better. If you ever wondered weather or not you can learn to troubleshoot electric problems, or you have been doing it for years, I recommend this book.
On a different note, the author is totally responsive too, I emailed him to share a scenario we had just worked through on the job, and I really didn't figure to get a response, Dan responded within the day.

rating 5.0. Excellent Must have book. I'm a ASE Certified Master Automotive Technician. I'm the go to diagnostics and electronics guy in my shop. And I LOVE this book.

Not only does it make some concepts easier for me to understand, but he gives me a far better way to explain some of these topics to my customers.

I've already purchased one for my grandfather, who also loved it, and we've discussed it a few times. He's learned a lot from Dan's methods of explaining, and I think anyone can benefit who works on cars, from a professional to a do-it-yourself-er.

I plan to buy one of these for every employee in my shop, and make it a mandatory read. I can't stress how easy Dan can make some of these topics.

rating 4.0. Some redundancy but easy to understand Overall a very nice book.  Single sided pages waste a lot of space which wouldn't be a big deal if the binding didn't continuously unravel.  When the binding uncurls itself, several holes on an end are exposed leaving very small bits of white paper on everything.  The content is useful with simple wording.  Often the same concept is restated (but that's not all bad!).  Almost entire book is presented as if it were someone notes on a notepad.  Odd, but the useable content is there. input:
rating 5.0. PM-1100 Great Product This is a solid kit. Instructions are easy to follow, all you need is a piece of plywood. Made a mobile base for my drill press, should have done this years ago. Easy to move drill press around the garage and very stable when lowered.
Bottom Line: Yes, I would recommend this to a friend

rating 3.0. Nice mobile base kit for the money. The Portamate PM -1100 Universal Mobile Base Kit is well constructed and fairly easy to assemble, the hardest part is putting the nylon bushings on the bolt that holds the locking levers, needle nose pliers come in handy here. The base is very low profile, maybe a 1/2" off the floor. I used a 24" x 34" x 3/4" piece of plywood for the mounting board . At first I thought I was missing a lot of hardware as there were only 8 nuts in the kit, but the bolts they provide actually screw into the corner brackets, they are threaded on the bottom plate. The other nuts are for the locking levers and the leveling feet.

The reason for only three stars and not four is that the kit was actually missing the hex nuts needed to mount the two casters on the lifting plates. I had to run up to a local box store to buy some 10mm 1.50 thread hex nuts to mount the casters. If you get the kit and the nuts for the casters are missing, just get hex nuts not nyloc nuts, you cannot tighten them on the caster because it swivels and there is no way to hold the bolt still to tighten it. There is also no room under the base to add lock washers and lock nuts if you so desire for added peace of mind, I know, I tried. You can fit regular hex nuts on the bit of thread that protrudes underneath and some blue Loctite, or thread the longer bolts through from underneath and put lock washers and nyloc nuts on the top side, I might still do this. I am using this for my Dewalt 788 Scroll Saw mounted on the Dewalt DW7880 Scroll Saw Stand.

rating 5.0. Quality mobile base I bought this to use with my 250 lbs table saw. It was easy to assemble. All the parts were there and they even sent longer anchor bolts to use with thicker dimension hardwood for the frame. There's a plate that sandwiches the frame material together to prevent tear out from tool movement. The wheel levers lift easily and with adjustment of the leveling legs the table sits firmly when lowered to the ground. They're a bit more pricey than others but I'd buy these again. They are worth the investment.
rating 5.0. Heavy Duty Enough for a Powermatic Band Saw! Very sturdy product when coupled with a piece of 3/4" birch plywood! It handles the weight of my large Powermatic bandsaw very well!

rating 5.0. Wow these are strong I thought these would be some thin gauged metal but tell you what, these things are built like a tank.  Super heavy and will support some pretty hefty equipment.  Good deal too for what this is for.

rating 5.0. Excellent product Man o man - an honest to goodness heavy duty well made easy to assemble product - a rarity these days to be sure. Bought a 3/4" piece of plywood at HD and had it cut to accommodate the 33" square base of my Craftsman band saw, marked and drilled the holes using 1/2 " drill bit, and slid into the easy to assemble corner brackets and tightened it all together. Voila! an easy to maneuver band saw.

I also took the lead from another reviewer and attached the 2 raise/lower levers together with a piece of sturdy but lightweight aluminum so as to better manipulate the raising and lowering process with a single move using one foot. Nice product, and one I would not hesitate to purchase again.

rating 5.0. Makes my bandsaw mobile. This set does exactly what I wanted it to, namely give my 200+ pound bandsaw some mobility. These are well built and provide a convenient and stable way to move equipment around. I have them on a drill press, Metal working lathe and a scroll saw. You will need to provide a piece of plywood for a base.

rating 5.0. Great Upgrade to Tool Base I have two tools that I wanted to mobilize, but had my doubts about how this item was going to work.I bought one set to try out. The item was out of stock when I ordered, but Amazon sent me an e-mail three days later saying it was being shipped. I didn't expect this so quickly, but was pleased. Out of the box, these appeared to be heavy duty, all metal construction (except for wheels). I decided to install it on the largest tool, a cast iron router table requiring a 30x40 piece of plywood. If that didn't work, I knew I could recut the plywood and use in on my smaller bandsaw. Assembly was easy. The short bolts were perfect for the 3/4" plywood which is sufficient to support the table. (If I had to span much more than 40", I might think about using another 1/2 to 3/4 sheet of plywood-longer bolts are provided for that). The heavy tool rolls easily around my shop when I want it out of the way and is steady and solid when I am working on it. I was so impressed, I ordered a second unit for the bandsaw.

rating 5.0. Moves heavy bandsaw easily Brilliant design. Super low profile adds maybe one inch to the working height of a tool mounted on this mobile base. Very thick plywood would add a bit more height of course.

In theory a piece of plywood that is one inch bigger in each direction from the base of the tool is an adequate size. However, look at the bolt pattern for securing the tool to the mobile base. Bolting the tool to the base is essential. The four corner plates that mount on the top of the mobile base are substantial. Best not to be drilling holes there. Make sure the plywood base is long enough or wide enough to leave the tool's bolt holes unobstructed.

Check the included parts before starting. Of course we do that every time, right? This time I noticed there was a full set of 12 of the machine screws that hold the four top corner plates to the plywood and then to the threaded holes in the four base corner pieces with wheels. However it seems there should have been two sets of 12, one longer, one shorter, to adapt to the thickness of the wood used. In a rush to finish my project I just bought twelve of the shorter 5/16" machine screws I needed.

The directions say to drill 1/2" holes in the plywood. I drilled 3/8" holes since the screws are 5/16". If doing again I would drill the 1/2" holes just to save some assembly time.

I'm using this mobile base for a top-heavy Jet bandsaw that weighs 200 pounds. Everything moves well. Again, essential to bolt the tool to the base.

Also notable: CUSTOMER SERVICE. Two phone calls, both answered by a real person, with answers. Awesome.
rating 5.0. Great value Excellent service and price. I am very pleased. Thank you

rating 5.0. Well Built Product The Bora Portamate Mobile Base Kit is a well built product.  Still in the stage of mounting the kit, but I don't foresee any problems with the product.  Will update if necessary.  FYI, I do plan to purchase more Bora products based on the build quality of this kit.

rating 4.0. Pretty good unit... I have a few different mobile bases. A couple HTC, a couple OEM. I got this for an antique scroll saw I restored. Putting the trucks together was straightforward, with only a little bit of interpretation of the instructions. Accurately measuring the base of the tool for the plywood panel is important. Too small, and you will be cutting another piece. And of course, consider what you are making mobile. Trying to put a vintage Oliver 12" jointer on this is a certain failure.

Comparing this to the HTC, I think the levers that lower the wheels are better on this one, as they are metal instead of the plastic ones on the HTC. Plastic typically doesn't wear well over time in this kind of application, as they are effectively bearing the entire weight of the tool and base when the unit is lifted. So when the HTC levers eventually fail, I'll probably replace it with one of these.

rating 5.0. Wish I had discovered this before I bought a different mobile base! I originally bought the HTC2000 Adjustible Mobil Base kit and extra extender kit to make a base for my compound miter saw which is on a stand that is 28” wide by 63” long. The HTC2000 and extender kit went together fine and moved the saw around great. The problem I had with that arrangement was every time I went to use the saw I tripped over the mobile base frame because it was a giant rectangle frame. The stand that the saw sits on has upside down V-shaped flared out legs. Between the legs the saw sits on a pair of parallel bars that connect to the top of the Upside down V - so they are more centered than the full width of the legs at the floor level. I kept trying to figure out how to modify that mobile base unit so the front part of the frame wouldn’t be right where you need to stand to use the saw.

I started looking around on Amazon again at mobile bases and discovered the Portamate PM-1100 so decided to give it a try. Glad I did! I just finished assembling it and it works great. I used some 1x6 pressure treated boards for the 28” short ends and a 63” piece of 2x4 down the center forming large “H” configuration. I connected the boards into the “H” using some carriage bolts. Plopped the saw on top of the big “H” and it works perfectly! Rolls around very nicely and no more tripping over the frame.

A few suggestions for assembly...when you are assembling the corner pieces, drill the holes a little bigger than the size of the bolt. If you have a drill press use that. I didn’t get some of my holes straight at first and had trouble lining up the holes for the top/bottom plates until I reamed out the holes I drilled crooked. Also it would have been great to have a second set of hands available especially when I was fighting the crooked holes but I eventually used the bench vice and that helped. Plan out and then double check your plan where you want the swivel wheels to be - both on the short end or on the lon end. I thought I had it figured out until it was all together and I realized for such a long piece it would be better to have the swivel wheels on the short end. So After struggling with all my crooked holes, I had to take it all apart and put it together again with the wheels in different corners. Went together easier the second time! Lastly, be aware that there isn’t much clearance under the bottom plate, only about a half inch, so if your floor isn’t level or you are moving a long piece down a signtly sloped floor, you will scrape bottom.

I plan to buy another Portamate PM-1100 for my lathe and configure it like I did for the miter saw, just not sure what to do with the HTC2000 now but I’m sure in time I will find a tool that needs wheels. Good luck - this was a good project for me, a novice just setting up a shop.
